summary: Soaring Sugaree and fire‑filled second set with tight segues
blurb: The show bursts open with a soaring Sugaree and a monster China > Rider > Promised Land sequence, then rolls into a high‑energy second set packed with Shakedown Street, Althea and an unforgettable UJB sandwich. Jerry’s lead work is described as pure soul while Brent adds fire on the keys and the dual drummers drive the rhythmic backbone.
review: On September 6, 1980 at the State Fairgrounds in Lewiston, Maine, the Dead delivered one of those rare late‑80s nights that still feels fresh. The first set builds momentum with an Alabama Getaway kickoff, but the real highlight arrives early with a Sugaree that reviewers call “soaring” and a relentless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider → The Promised Land marathon that showcases Jerry’s “pure soul” guitar work and Brent’s B‑3 chops.  The second set is a masterclass in Dead chemistry: Shakedown Street grooves into Lost Sailor, Saint Of Circumstance and Althea before the classic Playing In The Band → Uncle John’s Band sandwich spins into a thunderous Drums/Space segment that segues into Not Fade Away → The Wheel → another UJB → P.I.T.B. climax, all capped by a jubilant Sugar Magnolia.  Across dozens of audience‑FOB and matrix tapes the performance is uniformly praised – Jerry “tears up” every tune, Phil’s bass thunders like an earth‑shaker, Brent fuels the fire, and Mickey and Bill lock in with “great drums” that push the jams forward.  The best listening experience comes from the crystal‑clear front‑of‑house recording (gd1980-09-04.fob.nak700.rolfe.miller.93783.sbeok.flac24), which captures the band’s energy and the crowd’s excitement without the occasional low‑end boom of other audience sources.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All analyzed recordings are marked positive; reviewers repeatedly use terms like "soaring", "pure soul", "fire‑filled" and "legendary" for the performance, with multiple sources highlighting the same standout songs (Sugaree, China>Rider>Promised Land, Shakedown Street, UJB sandwich). No negative sentiment appears across recordings, just a few minor recording‑quality notes, justifying a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear front‑of‑house FOB tape with excellent ambience and balance; captures the band’s energy without dropouts, making it the most enjoyable listening experience.

key_highlights: Sugaree solo that “soars” and showcases Jerry’s soulful lead work
The marathon China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider → The Promised Land sequence
Second‑set UJB sandwich (Playing In The Band → Uncle John's Band) leading into a powerful Wheel climax
Shakedown Street groove with Brent’s B3 fire and Phil’s thundering bass

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers soaring, soulful solos throughout – Sugaree, the China>Rider>Promised Land marathon and the second‑set jams are repeatedly praised as “pure Jerry soul” and “tearing up” each tune.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is described as thundering, earth‑shaking and a solid foundation; a few audience tapes note extra low‑end boominess but overall his playing drives the band forward.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s rhythm work stays tight; his vocals are noted as hoarse yet passionate, with one reviewer mentioning his volume briefly drowning Jerry on Sugaree.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s keyboard chops add fire to the set – B3 tones and tasteful fills are highlighted across multiple recordings, especially during Shakedown Street and the UJB sandwich.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart contributes a powerful rhythmic backbone; reviewers praise his driving presence alongside Bill throughout the jams.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming is called “great drums,” with solid post‑drums space sections that enhance the Rhythm Devils segment.
